lessons.title: try-with-resources in Detail
lessons.summary: Learn the mechanics of the try-with-resources statement in detail. Understand the difference between AutoCloseable and Closeable, how to declare multiple resources, and the order in which resources are closed (reverse order of declaration).

contents.title: Overview of try-with-resources
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Detailed rules of **try-with-resources**
- Effectively final resource [[variables]] since Java 9
- Managing multiple resources simultaneously

Let's precisely understand the specification of automatic resource management.

contents.title: AutoCloseable and Closeable
contents.body:
The [[AutoCloseable]] [[interface]] belongs to the java.lang package and declares the close() [[method]].
The Closeable interface belongs to the java.io package and is a sub-interface of AutoCloseable.
AutoCloseable's close() can throw Exception, whereas Closeable's close() can only throw IOException.
Only [[classes]] that implement AutoCloseable can be used with **try-with-resources**.
Since Closeable [[inherits]] from AutoCloseable, it can naturally be used with [[try-with-resources]].
Since Java 9, effectively final variables declared outside the try block can also be used in try-with-resources.
contents.note: Closeable is a sub-interface of AutoCloseable. Be aware of questions on the type hierarchy.

contents.title: Multiple Resources and Close Order
contents.body:
In **try-with-resources**, multiple resources can be declared separated by semicolons.
Resources are closed in the reverse order of their declaration (LIFO like a [[stack]]).
This mechanism ensures proper resolution of dependencies between resources.
For example, a statement created on top of a previously opened connection should be closed first.
Even if an [[exception]] occurs during close, the closing of other resources is still attempted.

contents.title: Improvements Since Java 9
contents.body:
Since Java 9, effectively final variables declared outside the try block can be placed directly in the try parentheses.
This simplification makes the code cleaner when managing pre-created resources with **try-with-resources**.
The variable must not have been reassigned (effectively final).
Passing a reassigned variable to the try parentheses results in a [[compile]] error.
contents.note: The Java 9 improvement is also tested on exams. Make sure you thoroughly understand the conditions for effectively final.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Resource Management
contents.body:
On the Gold exam, questions about resource close order are frequently asked.
Remember that resources are closed in the reverse order of declaration.
Resources are closed even if an exception occurs within the try block.
If an exception occurs during resource declaration (in the [[constructor]]), only the resources that have been created up to that point are closed.
The catch/[[finally]] block is executed after the resources have been closed.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Resources are created in the order X → Y, but they are closed in the reverse order of declaration, so Y → X. The output is "X open", "Y open", "use", "Y close", "X close".

choices.text: X open → Y open → use → Y close → X close
X open → Y open → use → X close → Y close
Y open → X open → use → X close → Y close
X open → Y open → X close → Y close → use

quizzes.question: Which of the following is correct about the relationship between AutoCloseable and Closeable?
quizzes.explanation: Closeable is a sub-interface of AutoCloseable. AutoCloseable belongs to the java.lang package, and its close() method can throw Exception. On the other hand, Closeable can only throw IOException.

choices.text: Closeable is a sub-interface of AutoCloseable
AutoCloseable is a sub-interface of Closeable
There is no inheritance relationship between them

quizzes.question: A resource that can be used with try-with-resources must implement the ___ interface.
quizzes.explanation: Resources managed by try-with-resources must implement the AutoCloseable interface. Since Closeable is a sub-interface of AutoCloseable, classes implementing Closeable can also be used.

lessons.title: Suppressed Exceptions
lessons.summary: Learn about the suppressed exception mechanism in try-with-resources. Understand the behavior when exceptions occur in both the try block and close(), and how to use the getSuppressed() method.

contents.title: What Are Suppressed Exceptions?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The mechanism and retrieval of suppressed exceptions
- What happens when exceptions occur in both the try block and close()
- Retrieving exception information with getSuppressed()

Let's understand the mechanism that ensures exceptions are never "swallowed."

contents.title: How Suppressed Exceptions Work
contents.body:
If exception A occurs in the try block and exception B occurs in close(), exception A is thrown and exception B is suppressed.
Suppressed exceptions can be retrieved using the primary exception's getSuppressed() [[method]].
getSuppressed() returns a Throwable[].
If exceptions occur in the close() of multiple resources, they are all added as suppressed exceptions.
If no exception occurs in the try block and an exception occurs only in close(), that close() exception is thrown directly (as a regular exception, not suppressed).
contents.note: Remember the priority: the try block exception is primary, and the close() exception is suppressed.

contents.title: Multiple Resources and Suppressed Exceptions
contents.body:
When multiple resources are declared, exceptions from each resource's close() all become suppressed exceptions.
Resources are closed in reverse order, so close() is called starting from the last declared resource.
Each close() exception is added to the getSuppressed() [[array]].
Even if no exception occurs in the try block, if the first close() throws an exception, that becomes the primary exception, and the remaining close() exceptions are suppressed.

contents.title: The addSuppressed Method
contents.body:
The Throwable class defines the addSuppressed(Throwable) method, which allows programmers to manually add suppressed exceptions.
getSuppressed() returns a Throwable[] with elements stored in the order they were added.
In **try-with-resources**, the JVM automatically calls addSuppressed() to add suppressed exceptions.
Attempting to add itself as a suppressed exception throws an IllegalArgumentException.
contents.note: addSuppressed() can be used manually, but it is normally used through the automatic processing of **try-with-resources**.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Suppressed Exceptions
contents.body:
On the Gold exam, the execution order and exception relationships between the try block, close(), catch, and [[finally]] are tested.
The execution order of **try-with-resources** is "try block → close() (reverse order) → catch → finally".
When exceptions occur in both the try block and close(), the catch receives the try block's exception.
If an exception occurs only in close(), that exception is passed directly to catch.
Also watch out for questions comparing with traditional try-finally.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: The "try" exception occurs in the try block, and the "close" exception occurs in close(). Since the try block exception becomes the primary, e.getMessage() returns "try". The close() exception is added as a suppressed exception, so getSuppressed().length is 1.

quizzes.question: In try-with-resources, when exception A occurs in the try block and exception B occurs in close(), which description is correct?
quizzes.explanation: In try-with-resources, the try block exception is treated as the primary exception, and the close() exception is added as a suppressed exception to the primary. The catch block receives exception A, and exception B can be retrieved with getSuppressed().

choices.text: Exception A is thrown, and exception B becomes a suppressed exception of A
Exception B is thrown, and exception A becomes a suppressed exception of B
Only exception A is thrown, and exception B is lost
Only exception B is thrown, and exception A is lost

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: The try block executes normally and "run" is output. Then resources are closed in reverse order, outputting "B closed" and "A closed". Since no exception occurs, the catch is not executed. Finally, "finally" is output.

choices.text: run → B closed → A closed → finally
run → A closed → B closed → finally
run → B closed → A closed → caught → finally
run → finally → B closed → A closed

quizzes.question: Complete the code to define a custom exception class.
quizzes.explanation: Custom exceptions are created by extending Exception. Use super(msg) to pass the message to the parent constructor.

quizzes.question: Arrange the correct flow of exception handling in the proper order.
quizzes.explanation: Catch blocks must list more specific exceptions first. Placing Exception before a specific type causes a compile error. finally comes last.

lessons.title: Assertions
lessons.summary: Learn about Java assertions (assert statement). Understand the assert statement syntax, how to enable/disable (-ea flag), and appropriate use cases (precondition/postcondition checking).

contents.title: What Are Assertions?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The assert statement syntax and usage
- Enabling [[assertions]] (-ea option)
- Where assertions should and should not be used

Learn about assertions as a bug detection tool during development.

contents.title: Assert Statement Syntax
contents.body:
The assert statement has two forms.
Form 1: "assert condition;" — If the condition is false, an AssertionError without a message is thrown.
Form 2: "assert condition : errorMessage;" — If the condition is false, an AssertionError with the specified message is thrown.
Any expression can be specified as the error message, which is converted to a string using toString().
The condition expression must be of boolean type.
AssertionError is a subclass of Error and should normally not be caught.
contents.note: A message can be specified after the colon (:) in the assert condition. This is a frequently tested syntax on the Gold exam.

contents.title: Enabling and Disabling Assertions
contents.body:
Assertions are disabled by default. To enable them, specify an option at JVM startup.
-ea (or -enableassertions): Enables assertions.
-da (or -disableassertions): Disables assertions.
Enabling/disabling can be controlled on a per-package basis.
System class assertions are enabled with -esa.

contents.title: Appropriate Use Cases for Assertions
contents.body:
Assertions can be used for precondition checks of [[methods]], but they are inappropriate for argument checks on public methods.
For public method argument checks, use exceptions like IllegalArgumentException.
Assertions are appropriate for private method argument checks (since callers are limited to the class itself).
They are suitable for verifying postconditions — confirming that a method's result is as expected.
They can also be used for class invariant verification.
They are useful for verifying unreachable points in control flow (such as the default case in a [[switch statement]]).
contents.note: The reason not to use assert for public method argument checks is that the check is not performed when assertions are disabled.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Assertions
contents.body:
On the Gold exam, questions about whether assert statement syntax (message after the colon) is correct are common.
The assert condition must be boolean, and a void-returning method call cannot be used in the message part.
Assertions should not be used in operations with side effects (since they are not executed when disabled).
Specifying a void-type method in the message part of an assert statement causes a [[compile]] error.
Remember that AssertionError [[inherits]] from the Error class and is not checked.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a correct description about the use of assertions?
quizzes.explanation: Assertions are disabled by default and are enabled by specifying the -ea option at JVM startup. When not enabled, assert statements are ignored. For public method argument checks, exceptions should be thrown instead of using assertions.

choices.text: Assertions are disabled by default and enabled with the -ea option
Assertions are enabled by default and disabled with the -da option
Assertions are ideal for argument checks in public methods
AssertionError is a subclass of Exception

quizzes.question: In which of the following scenarios is the use of assertions appropriate?
quizzes.explanation: Since private methods are called only from within the class, assertions are appropriate for precondition checks on their arguments. For public method argument checks, exceptions like IllegalArgumentException should be used. User input validation should always be checked, so assertions are inappropriate.

choices.text: Precondition check on a private method's arguments
Argument check on a public method
Validating input values from the user

quizzes.question: Select the error contained in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: A void-type expression cannot be used in the second expression (after the colon) of an assert statement. Since System.out.println() returns void, this results in a compile error. An expression that has a value, such as a string literal or variable, must be specified.

choices.text: A void-type expression (println) is used in the message part of the assert statement
The assert condition is of int type
assert statements cannot be used inside the main method
assert statements require a catch block

quizzes.question: Select all subclasses of RuntimeException.
quizzes.explanation: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, and ClassCastException are unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException). IOException and SQLException are checked exceptions.

lessons.title: Multi-catch and Rethrow
lessons.summary: Learn the details of Gold-level multi-catch syntax and exception rethrow patterns. Understand rethrowing with more specific types and the exception wrapping pattern.

contents.title: Multi-catch in Detail
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Multi-catch (catch(A | B e)) syntax and constraints
- [[Type inference]] during rethrow (throw e)
- Evolution of [[exception]] handling since Java 7

Master advanced exception handling techniques.

contents.title: Multi-catch Constraints
contents.body:
Having an [[inheritance]] relationship between exception types specified in multi-catch causes a [[compile]] error.
For example, catch (IOException | Exception e) is invalid (IOException is a sub[[class]] of Exception).
The exception [[variable]] in a multi-catch is implicitly final and cannot be reassigned.
The exception variable in a single catch clause can be reassigned.
The type of the multi-catch exception variable is treated as the common parent type of the specified exception types.
contents.note: The fact that multi-catch variables are final is a frequently tested point. Make sure to remember it.

contents.title: Exception Rethrow
contents.body:
Since Java 7, the compiler can more precisely infer the type of an exception being rethrown within a catch block.
Even when catching Exception in a catch clause, the [[method]]'s throws declaration can be more specific based on the types actually thrown in the try block.
However, this type inference only works when the exception variable has not been reassigned.
If the exception variable is reassigned, the compiler can no longer perform accurate type inference.

contents.title: Exception Wrapping Pattern
contents.body:
Exception wrapping is a pattern where a caught exception is wrapped in a different exception and rethrown.
The original exception is passed as a cause to the new exception's [[constructor]].
This allows using exceptions at the appropriate level of abstraction between layers while preserving the original exception information.
The original exception can be retrieved using the getCause() method.
This pattern is also very important in practice.
contents.note: Exception wrapping is an important pattern for preventing lower-layer implementation details from leaking to upper layers.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Multi-catch and Rethrow
contents.body:
Multi-catch constraints (prohibition of inheritance relationships, finality of variables) are almost certainly tested.
Remember that type inference during rethrow does not work when the exception variable has been reassigned.
For the exception wrapping pattern, understand that the original exception can be retrieved with getCause().
The initCause() method can also set the cause exception later (but only once).
Familiarize yourself with the pattern of processing order within catch blocks (log output → wrap → rethrow).

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: The "inner" exception is thrown in the inner try and caught. A new "outer" exception is created with the original "inner" exception set as the cause, and rethrown. In the outer catch, getMessage() of the received exception returns "outer", and getCause().getMessage() returns "inner".

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a correct description about multi-catch syntax?
quizzes.explanation: The exception variable in a multi-catch is implicitly final, so it cannot be reassigned. Specifying exception types with an inheritance relationship causes a compile error. Multi-catch has been available since Java 7.

choices.text: The multi-catch exception variable is implicitly final and cannot be reassigned
The multi-catch exception variable can be reassigned
Exception types with an inheritance relationship can be specified in multi-catch

quizzes.question: In the exception wrapping pattern, the ___ method is used to retrieve the original exception.
quizzes.explanation: In the exception wrapping pattern, the original exception is passed as a cause to the new exception's constructor. The wrapped original exception can be retrieved using the getCause() method.
